text: The surgeon begins the procedure by identifying the inferior epigastric vessels and starting dissection at the medial umbilical ligament, progressing laterally to address the indirect inguinal hernia on the right side.
The surgeon attempts to enter the preperitoneal plane, which appears thicker than usual, resulting in fused tissue layers. The surgeon accepts both layers and proceeds laterally.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz on top of the fat layer, pushing the fat laterally over the underlying nerves.
The surgeon attempts to re-establish the preperitoneal plane from the undersurface of the flap, but the tissue is too thin. The procedure continues medially, working within the supravesicular space.
The surgeon switches to an oblique angle view, using electrocautery to buzz on top of the fat layer, progressing towards the infra-upper gastric region.

The surgeon switches to an oblique angle and uses cautery to buzz directly on the hernia sac, pushing surrounding fat away. This maneuver progresses towards the end of the sac, revealing the vas deferens coming into view.

The surgeon maintains a superior and medial position relative to the fat, using cautery on the hernia sac. The vas deferens is visible, with the preserved deferential vessel running medial to it, contributing to the testicular blood supply.

The surgeon gently pulls the hernia sac medially while applying cautery in small increments, allowing the tissue to fall away. The sac is maneuvered close to the deferential vessel, with the spermatic cord visibly preserved and free of any cord lipoma.
The surgeon identifies the right iliac vein and proceeds with a more assertive medial dissection, focusing on the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs sweeping motions to dissect around the hernia sac, revealing the iliopubic tract. The hernia sac is not large, and there is no evidence of a cord lipoma.

The surgeon performs high lateral fixation using two ovicral sutures, followed by Cooper's fixation with the same suture technique. The undersurface of the muscle is clearly visible during the procedure.
The surgeon observes the internal oblique muscle, confirming its integrity, and proceeds with a typical flap closure technique.
